questions.question: What does Monte Carlo need that dynamic programming doesn't, and vice versa?
options: MC needs a GPU; DP needs a CPU
MC needs only the ability to SAMPLE episodes — no model; DP needs the full transition model P(s'|s,a) but gets exact backups — the shift trades higher variance for vastly broader applicability
Both need the same things
MC needs labels; DP needs rewards
questions.explanation: A robot can't analytically compute pixel distributions after a torque; an LLM can't enumerate all continuations. Every later algorithm — TD, Q-learning, REINFORCE, PPO, GRPO — is a Monte Carlo estimator at heart.

questions.question: What distinguishes first-visit from every-visit MC?
options: First-visit is biased, every-visit isn't

When an episode visits state s multiple times, first-visit counts only the return from the first visit (simpler analysis, iid samples) while every-visit counts all of them (more data per episode, typically faster convergence) — both unbiased in the limit
First-visit only works on grids
Every-visit requires a model
questions.explanation: Both estimate V^π(s) = E[G_t | s_t = s] by averaging observed returns — the choice is a data-efficiency vs analysis-simplicity trade.

questions.question: Why is the incremental-mean rewrite V_new = V_old + α(target − V_old) so important?
options: It saves a negligible amount of memory
Swapping α = 1/n for a constant step size yields a non-stationary estimator that tracks a changing policy — that single move is the entire jump from MC to TD and to every modern RL update rule
It makes MC exact like DP
It eliminates the need for episodes
questions.explanation: Every RL update you'll ever write has this shape: old estimate plus step size times (target minus old estimate). Only the target changes between algorithms.

questions.question: Why does exploration become a problem in MC, and what are the three classic fixes?
options: MC explores too much by default

MC only sees states the policy visits — a deterministic π leaves whole regions at zero value forever; fixes: exploring starts (random initial (s,a), unrealistic for robots), ε-greedy (random action with probability ε), and off-policy MC via importance sampling
Exploration is only a DP problem
Random seeds fix it automatically
questions.explanation: DP touched every state by enumeration; sampling methods must earn their coverage. Off-policy MC's importance sampling is high-variance but bridges to replay-buffer methods like DQN.

questions.question: What is Monte Carlo Control, and when does it converge?
options: A variance-reduction technique

Sampling-based policy iteration: run π for an episode, update Q(s,a) from observed returns, make π ε-greedy w.r.t. Q, repeat — converging to Q* and π* with probability 1 if every pair is visited infinitely often and α satisfies Robbins-Monro
Running MC on multiple seeds
A method for continuous actions only
questions.explanation: It's GPI with a sampled evaluator — the same evaluate-improve loop as policy iteration, with rollout averages standing in for exact Bellman backups.
